Human engraftment was assessed at 12 weeks post-transplantation as described above for CB and cells cryopreserved for downstream analysis. After mouse depletion (Miltenyi), samples were stained for FACS (see Supplementary Table 22) to pre-sort for hCD45+hCD34+hCD19−hCD33− HSPCs and hCD45+CD33+CD34−CD19− myeloid cells on a BD Fusion instrument. The NOC153 bone marrow sample was included as a technical control for each sort of a xenografted sample. Pre-sorted subpopulations underwent single-cell index sorting on a Sony MA900 into 384-well plates containing 3 µl lysis buffer for downstream library preparation and computational analysis as previously described with the comparable samples from the original TARGET-seq+ dataset52.

GSEA analysis in human acute myeloid leukaemia

Comparison of BoneMarrowMap-defined HSC–MPP from acute myeloid leukaemia samples versus healthy controls. scRNA-seq data generated from six studies with (1) clinical information on DNMT3A and TET2 status, and (2) internal healthy control samples, were included32,103,104. This constituted 91 acute myeloid leukaemia samples and 16 healthy control samples. From these 91 samples, 15 were clinically annotated as DNMT3A-MUT, 18 were clinically annotated as TET2-MUT, 35 were clinically annotated as WT for both DNMT3A and TET2 (double negative), and the remaining were not annotated for DNMT3A or TET2 mutation status. Pseudobulk profiles were obtained at the level of the HSC–MPP populations from each patient sample, and differential expression was performed with DESeq2 between acute myeloid leukaemia HSC–MPP and healthy HSC–MPP while controlling for study as a covariate.

Myeloid and progenitor scRNA-seq QC and preprocessing

Raw sequencing data were pre-processed with CellRanger-ARC (v2.0.0) and aligned to GRCh38. The RNA feature count matrix for each sample was corrected for ambient RNA contamination using SoupX (v1.6.2)79 with default parameters. SoupX-corrected RNA counts were processed using Seurat (v4.3.0) and the following quality control-filtering thresholds were applied to each sample: percentage of mitochondrial genes (pct_mito) < 10% and number of unique RNA transcripts detected (nFeature_SoupX) > 300. After initial quality control, single-cell transcriptomes were normalized by scran (v1.20.1), followed by variable feature selection, scaling and PCA reduction. Batch correction with Harmony (v0.1.1)85 was performed with sex as a batch variable. A UMAP reduction was performed using 20 Harmony-corrected principal components, and a neighbourhood graph was constructed using the top 30 neighbours for each cell. Louvain clustering was performed at a resolution of 1.4, and clusters were condensed by concordance for predicted cell-type annotations upon mapping to the BoneMarrowMap32. Neutrophil progenitors were validated by evaluating expression for MPO and ELANE.

Inferring genetic clonal groups using SoupOrCell

To exploit the extensive genetic heterogeneity within the 20-week xenograft data, we utilized SoupOrCell (v2.5), a tool that performs unsupervised clustering of genetic polymorphisms from the raw sequencing data within a single-cell experiment to capture this genetic heterogeneity as barcodes for lineage tracing. To combine the raw sequencing data from the CD34+CD38−CD45RA− HSPCs with newly generated scRNA-seq data from CD34+CD38− progenitors and CD33+ mature myeloid cells isolated from these same xenografts, we merged the gene expression bam files from CellRanger-ARC (v2.0.0) using pysam (v0.15.1). Merged bam files were sorted and indexed using samtools (v1.17) with default parameters. SoupOrCell was iteratively run across a parameter search from k 1–30, and the optimal number of genetic clonal groups was evaluated using an elbow plot of the total log likelihood and concordance with predicted sex assignments.

Clonal groups were assigned as HSC-I dominant, HSC-iM dominant and nonspecific based on cell composition within the HSC pool. Specifically, clonal groups with more than 95% HSC-iM or HSC-I within the HSC pool were assigned as HSC-iM dominant and HSC-I dominant, respectively. Remaining clonal groups (HSC-iM proportions ranging from 12% to 60% in the HSC pool) were assigned as nonspecific. For differential expression analysis between HSC-I-dominant and HSC-iM-dominant clonal groups, pseudobulk profiles were constructed from each cell type within each clonal group within each treatment condition, and DESeq2 (v1.32.0) was applied to pseudobulk profiles comparing HSC-iM-specific clones against HSC-I-specific clones for each cell type. GSEA was performed using the fgsea (v1.18.0) package. Before differential expression, pseudobulk profiles consisting of less than five cells were filtered out.

Donor-level associations between HSCs and monocytes

To indirectly infer associations between transcriptional features of monocytes against HSC-iM program enrichment from upstream HSCs, scRNA-seq data from three scRNA-seq datasets of bulk mononuclear cells within BoneMarrowMap32 were used105,106,107. First, HSCs and CD14 monocytes from each donor were collapsed into pseudobulk profiles, and only healthy donors with 5 or more HSCs as well as 5 or more monocytes were included, leading to 32 donors used for this analysis. For each donor, the mean enrichment score for the HSC-iM program (top 200 genes scored by AUCell) within the HSC pool was obtained. Next, using the pseudobulk transcriptome for the monocytes from each donor, DESeq2 was applied to regress the monocyte transcriptomes against a continuous variable representing enrichment of the HSC-iM program within the HSC pool. Test statistics and P values were obtained for each gene within the monocyte transcriptome, and these statistics were used to construct a rank list for downstream GSEAs. Positive-test statistics translate to higher expression of a given gene in the monocytes of donors with greater HSC-iM enrichment within their upstream HSCs.

Clone-level associations between HSCs and monocytes

To more directly infer associations between transcriptional features of monocytes against HSC-iM program enrichment from upstream HSCs, we utilized a native lineage-tracing dataset integrating mitochondrial DNA variant-based lineage tracing with concomitant multi-omic profiling of HSCs8. Pre-processed single-cell transcriptomes from their dataset were projected onto BoneMarrowMap32 to identify HSCs and CD14 monocytes. Next, among mitochondrial DNA-defined clones assigned from their study, we identified clones with at least three HSCs and at least three CD14 monocytes. This resulted in retention of 213 clones spanning 13 donors, which was used for association analysis. For each clone, the mean enrichment score for the HSC-iM program (top 200 genes scored by AUCell) within the HSC pool was obtained. Next, using the pseudobulk transcriptomes for the monocytes from each clone, DESeq2 was applied to regress the monocyte transcriptomes against a continuous variable representing enrichment of the HSC-iM program within the HSC pool. Test statistics and P values were obtained for each gene within the monocyte transcriptome, and these statistics were used to construct a rank list for downstream GSEAs. Positive-test statistics transplate to higher expression of a given gene in the monocytes of clones with greater HSC-iM enrichment within their upstream HSCs.

BM xenograft TARGET-seq+ data processing

Raw sequencing data were preprocessed with the TARGET-seq+ pipeline (https://github.com/asgerjakobsen/TARGET-seq-plus) as previously described52,108. In brief, transcriptome data were preprocessed with a custom pipeline (https://github.com/asgerjakobsen/TARGET-seq-plus-RNA) to trim and demultiplex reads into single-cell barcodes using cutadapt (v3.4) and then mapped them to the hg38 reference genome and ERCC92 transcripts with STAR (v2.7.10a) using the GENCODE v38 reference gene annotation. Genotyping amplicon data were demultiplexed and processed to generate tables of allelic counts, which were used to call cell genotypes as previously described52 using single-cell data from the NOC153 primary bone marrow sample as WT controls. Finally, FACS indexing, genotyping and transcriptome data were integrated for downstream analysis in Seurat (v5.3.0).

The following quality control filters were applied to the starting dataset of 10,640 cells: minimum of 5,000 RNA reads per cell, minimum of 1,000 genes per cell, maximum percentage of mitochondrial reads = 15% and maximum percentage of ERCC reads = 65%, leaving 8,998 cells remaining. The filtered data were integrated with the 13,247 primary bone marrow cells from the original TARGET-seq+ dataset52. Genes expressed in fewer than 10 cells were removed and reads were normalized by scran (v1.36.0) using the pool normalization method with prior clustering, followed by variable feature selection, scaling and PCA reduction. Batch correction with Harmony (v1.2.3) was performed with sample donor and experimental batch as batch variables. A UMAP reduction was performed using 30 Harmony-corrected principal components. Louvain clustering was performed at a resolution of 1. Clusters were annotated and condensed by concordance with predicted cell-type annotations upon mapping to the BoneMarrowMap along with the cell-type annotations from the original primary bone marrow dataset and immunophenotypic profiles.

Inference of HSC origin in TARGET-seq+ data

To identify HSC-I-derived and HSC-iM-derived cells, we first defined a set of genes that distinguish the differentiation trajectories of HSC-I-dominant and HSC-iM-dominant primary bone marrow samples in the original TARGET-seq+ dataset. Within each cell type, single-cell transcriptomes from each donor were collapsed into pseudobulk profiles, and EdgeR was used to identify genes differentially expressed between HSC-I-dominant (n = 6) and HSC-iM-dominant (n = 4) samples. We selected genes that were differentially expressed at FDR < 0.10 within 5 or more cell types, generating a list of 702 genes. We then performed dimensionality reduction and clustering of HSPCs and monocytic cell types in the integrated TARGET-seq+ dataset using the SAM algorithm and the 702 HSC-I and HSC-iM trajectory genes. Louvain clustering was performed on the SAM embeddings at a resolution of 1.5, generating 15 clusters. These clusters were then annotated as being of HSC-I or HSC-iM origin based on the composition of primary bone marrow cells from HSC-I-dominant and HSC-iM-dominant samples and expression of the HSC-I and HSC-iM transcriptional programs.

For analysis of monocytic output in bone marrow xenografts, the number of early HSPC cells (HSC–MPP, LMPP, cycling progenitor and early GMP) and monocytic cells (late GMP, promonocytes and monocytes) were calculated separately for each sorted population (CD34+ and CD34–CD33+), and the CD34–CD33+ counts then multiplied by the ratio of CD34–CD33+:CD34+ cells in FACS-sorting gates to account for the size of each population in the xenograft sample. The frequency of monocytic cells was then divided by the frequency of early HSPCs to obtain a relative abundance.

scRNA-seq in the OHS to relate HSC-iM to IRS

Study population

CanPath is a population cohort of over 330,000 participants across 7 provinces in Canada, and has collected information on health, lifestyle, disease and environmental factors along with biological samples. Requests for access to CanPath data should be made through the data access portal (https://portal.canpath.ca/).

Sample selection

A modified version of the IRS (mIRS) was calculated for all participants using the sex-specific 5-year mortality risk coefficients for six values on a complete blood count: haematocrit, white blood cell count, platelet count, mean corpuscular volume, mean corpuscular haemoglobin concentration and red blood cell distribution width. The age component was excluded from the calculation to enable direct comparisons of blood cell profiles among participants of all ages, and the metabolic parameters were also excluded. The complete blood count-based mIRS has been shown to be associated with all-cause mortality in a cohort of approximately 30,000 participants109, as well as a randomized trial population of approximately 17,000 (ref. 110). Participants with any of the following self-reported diseases were excluded: high blood pressure, myocardial infarction, stroke, asthma, emphysema, chronic bronchitis, chronic obstructive pulmonary disorder, depression, diabetes, liver cirrhosis, chronic hepatitis, Crohn’s disease, ulcerative colitis, irritable bowel syndrome, eczema, systemic lupus erythematosus, psoriasis, multiple sclerosis, osteoporosis, arthritis and cancer. Samples were further filtered to retain only those with European ancestry, and those that were 45 years of age or younger, or 65 years of age or older, classified as young and aged, respectively. For extreme sampling, both the young and the aged groups were ranked according to their age and mIRS, and the top and bottom approximately 100 samples from each age group were retained for molecular phenotyping.

scRNA-seq library preparation, sequencing and data preprocessing

Tissue thawing medium (TTM) was prepared by mixing 10% FBS, 25 mM HEPES, 55 μM 2-mercaptoethanol and 1 mM sodium pyruvate, warmed to 37 °C. Buffy coat samples were thawed in a 37 °C water bath for approximately 2 min until a small ice crystal remained. Then, 1 ml TTM at 37 °C supplemented with 25 U ml−1 benzonase was added dropwise to the thawed sample. The cells were centrifuged at 600g for 8 min at room temperature. The supernatant was removed, and the cells were resuspended in 10 ml of TTM at 37 °C supplemented with 25 U ml–1 benzonase. The cells were incubated for 15 min at 37 °C. The cells were then centrifuged at 300g for 8 min at room temperature, and resuspended in 1 ml of FBS at 37 °C. The concentration of the cell suspension and viability was assessed using the TC20 Cell Counter (Bio-Rad) with trypan blue staining. The concentration of the cell suspension was adjusted to 1 × 106 cells per millilitre. The cells were left to recover for 30–60 min at 37 °C, after which CD45+ cells were isolated using MACS (Miltenyi Biotec) following the manufacturer’s protocol.

scRNA library preparation and sequencing were performed by the Genomics platform at the Ontario Institute for Cancer Research. From the isolated CD45+ single-cell suspension, 4 × 103 cells were used to construct libraries with the Chromium Single-Cell 3′ Reagent Kit with v2 chemistry (10X Genomics) following the manufacturer’s protocols. Single-indexed libraries were sequenced on the Illumina Novaseq platform with the following parameters: read 1 for 26 cycles, i7 index for 8 cycles and read 2 for 98 cycles. CellRanger (v3.0.0) --mkfastq was used to demultiplex BCL files and generate a fastq file for each scRNA-seq sample. Then, --count was used to align the fastq files to the hg38 reference, filter out barcodes for empty gel bead-in-emulsion, and generate a cell unique molecular identifier × gene matrix, which was used in downstream data analyses.

Single-cell data quality control, filtering and annotation

The following quality control filters were applied to the total starting dataset of 619,925 cells: a minimum of 300 genes per cell, a minimum of 10 cells per gene and maximum percentage of mitochondrial DNA = 20%, leaving 438,986 cells remaining. Samples were then downsampled within each risk group (young low risk, young high risk, aged low risk and aged high risk) such that the number of male and female individuals was equal within each risk group. Cell-type annotation was performed using BoneMarrowMap32. Annotations were grouped to form six cell-type categories: naive T cells, B cells, natural killer cells, dendritic cells, CD14 monocytes and CD16 monocytes. We removed memory T cells from analysis due to the potential for confounding effects introduced by T cell-specific memory, given its similarity to the HSC-iM program.

Scoring of HSC-I and HSC-iM signatures to predict IRS

Cell-type-specific pseudobulk profiles were created using decoupler (v2.0.2), with the ‘sum’ mode. Only samples with a minimum of 10 cells and 1,000 total counts were retained. The pseudobulked data were log normalized and scaled using default settings with scanpy.pp.normalize_total, scanpy.pp.log1p and scanpy.pp.scale. The top 200 genes in the HSC-I and HSC-iM signatures were scored on each cell-type-pseudobulk profile using scanpy.tl.score_genes. For each cell type, a generalized linear model was constructed to predict high risk or low risk (high or low risk ~ HSC-I or HSC-iM score + batch + sex + age), and regressions were conducted separately among young samples and aged samples. P values from each regression were adjusted for multiple testing using the Benjamini–Hochberg procedure, within the young and aged groups, using a significance threshold of FDR < 0.05.

Statistical testing and data presentation

Graphpad Prism (v9.2-10.6) was used to plot biological data and perform statistical testing as specified in the respective figure legends. Bioinformatic data were plotted and compared using R (v4.1.0-4.5.2 with packages ggplot2 v3.5.1-4.0.0, dplyr v1.1.4 and tidyverse v2.0.0) or Python3 (v3.11 with NumPy v2.2.5) as indicated in the respective sections in the Methods. Figure panels were assembled and most schematics were created using Microsoft Powerpoint (v16).

Reporting summary

Further information on research design is available in the Nature Portfolio Reporting Summary linked to this article.

item.title: 州医学委员会：医师助理未能达到护理标准- JoCo报告
item.description:
RALEIGH, N.C. — A physician assistant has been reprimanded by the North Carolina Medical Board following an investigation into prescribing practices, patient monitoring failures, and inadequate medical documentation involving multiple patients, including one who later died from an overdose.

According to a consent order approved April 20, the board reprimanded James John Rapalje, P.A., of Benson, N.C., who had practiced in Fayetteville and has held a North Carolina physician assistant license since 1976.

The investigation centered heavily on one long-term patient treated between 2018 and 2024 for attention deficit disorder and anxiety. Rapalje prescribed Xanax, a benzodiazepine, and Adderall, a stimulant medication, during that time.

The medical board said Rapalje failed to perform pill counts, drug screenings, or routinely check the North Carolina Controlled Substances Reporting System despite signs of possible drug diversion.

The patient later died from an overdose. Toxicology testing reportedly detected illicit drugs but did not detect the prescribed benzodiazepine medication.

According to the consent order, Rapalje failed to document formal diagnostic assessments or ongoing evaluations while continuing long-term prescriptions for benzodiazepines and stimulants.

The board also found Rapalje failed to properly monitor patients for dependence, misuse, adverse effects, or overdose risk and did not adequately document counseling patients about the dangers of long-term benzodiazepine use.

The consent order identified additional deficiencies involving four other patients. In several cases, Rapalje prescribed benzodiazepines as a first-line treatment without attempting alternative therapies. In two cases, narcotic pain medications were prescribed alongside benzodiazepines without proper documentation of risk assessments or alternatives.

One patient being treated for multiple myeloma and chronic pain received oxycodone prescriptions, but the board found there was no documentation showing reassessment of opioid effectiveness or monitoring for misuse and adverse effects.

The board also found Mr. Rapalje was being supervised by his son, a physician practicing in Vermont but also licensed in North Carolina. Investigators concluded the supervising physician did not provide meaningful oversight, particularly involving patients receiving opioid therapy.

Rapalje inactivated his physician assistant license on Feb. 9, 2026, according to the board.

Under the consent order, Rapalje admitted his conduct constituted unprofessional conduct under North Carolina law and waived his right to a hearing or appeal.

The name of the medical practice where Rapalje worked was not disclosed in the public filing.

The reprimand is now part of the public record and will be reported to national medical disciplinary databases, including the National Practitioner Data Bank.

Discover more from JoCo Report

Subscribe to get the latest posts sent to your email.

item.title: 在威斯康星州发现的mpox病例集群；预防资源- FOX6 News Milwaukee
item.description:
article

Colorized transmission electron micrograph of mpox virus particles (red/yellow) found within an infected cell (blue). (Photo by: NIH-NIAID/Image Point FR/BSIP/Universal Images Group via Getty Images)

MILWAUKEE - Following recent confirmed mpox cases in northern and southeastern Wisconsin, the Department of Health Services (DHS) is urging residents to take preventive measures.

While mpox has remained at low levels since 2022, recent surveillance data indicates a nationwide spike in infections over the past few weeks.

National data shows 535 confirmed mpox cases across the U.S. as of May 3, 2026. As of May 26, there have been five confirmed mpox cases in Wisconsin this year.

About Mpox

Dig deeper:

Mpox is not common but can be a serious virus spread through intimate, face-to-face contact, such as talking or breathing closely for long periods; sustained skin-to-skin contact; and contact with items that have been contaminated with the fluids or sores of a person with mpox.

Mpox usually causes a new, unexplained rash that develops into hard, round, fluid-filled blisters.

Some individuals with mpox may also experience fever, chills, muscle aches, or swollen lymph nodes.

The mpox rash typically develops within one to three days after fever. However, some people may experience rash or sores first.

FREE DOWNLOAD: Get breaking news alerts in the FOX LOCAL Mobile app for iOS or Android

Although the risk to the public is low, DHS encourages Wisconsinites at higher risk of mpox exposure to talk to a trusted health care provider about vaccination. 

Providers may recommend a person get vaccinated if they are:

  • Gay, bisexual, or a man who has sex with men including people who were assigned female at birth and have had more than on sex partner in the last six months.
  • Traveling and have a sexual exposure with someone in a different area.
  • In close contact with someone with mpox, including health care workers where exposure is possible.

Steps to prevent mpox infection

  • Know the symptoms of mpox.
  • Watch their and their partner's bodies for changes, such as rashes or skin lesions.
  • Have open and honest conversations with partners about mpox, STIs, and HIV.

The Source: The information in this post was provided by the Wisconsin Department of Health Services.

item.title: 6种经济实惠的低胆固醇食品储藏室主食- EatingWell
item.description:
• Pantry staples like beans and oats provide fiber to help lower LDL cholesterol naturally.
  • Olive oil and nuts offer heart-healthy fats that support cardiovascular health.
  • Making small, consistent changes to your diet and lifestyle can help improve heart health.

Eating to help improve your heart health may seem like a daunting—and costly—endeavor, but it doesn’t have to be. Some of the best foods for supporting healthy cholesterol levels are budget-friendly pantry staples that are versatile and convenient, and you may already have some of them on hand.

Cholesterol is one of many important puzzle pieces when it comes to heart health. While some cholesterol is necessary for normal body function, consistently high levels of LDL (“bad”) cholesterol can increase risk of cardiovascular disease over time. Your pantry can play an important role in mitigating this risk, especially if you keep it stocked with everyday staples like oats, extra-virgin olive oil and beans. These foods provide nutrients like soluble fiber and heart-healthy fats that can help lower your cholesterol.

We spoke to registered dietitians to round up this list of cholesterol-lowering pantry staples to stock up on, plus some simple ideas of what to make with them.

1. Black Beans

Black beans are a top pick when it comes to heart health, most notably because of their fiber content. They contain soluble fiber, which can help lower LDL (“bad”) cholesterol by binding to cholesterol in the digestive tract and helping remove it from the body before it’s absorbed into the bloodstream. “Regular inclusion of beans, around ¾ to 1 cup per day, is a practical, food-based approach to improving cholesterol,” says Johannah Katz, RD.

In addition to fiber, black beans also provide plant-based protein, iron and minerals like magnesium and potassium, notes Crystal Orozco, RD, which make them a nutritious addition to a balanced meal. “As a Latina dietitian, I love recommending black beans, pinto beans or lentils because they’re affordable, culturally familiar and easy to add to meals,” Orozco says. She recommends tossing beans into salads, or adding them to soups, tacos and burrito bowls.

Sarah Davis, M.S., RDN, shares another heart-healthy tip: “To reduce the sodium content, drain and rinse canned beans first.” For breakfast, Davis likes to heat them up in a pan with garlic and onion and add them to scrambled eggs for a fiber and protein boost to start her day.

2. Extra-Virgin Olive Oil

Extra-virgin olive oil contains heart-healthy fats and antioxidants that may help lower LDL cholesterol and protect the heart by reducing inflammation and keeping blood vessels healthy. Extra-virgin olive oil also contains compounds called polyphenols that may help support heart health in several ways. These compounds can help reduce the amount of cholesterol absorbed in the digestive tract while also supporting the body’s ability to remove excess cholesterol. Polyphenols may also help protect LDL cholesterol from becoming oxidized, a damaged form that is more likely to contribute to plaque buildup in the arteries.

Meaghan Ormsby, M.S., RDN, recommends drizzling olive oil over a charcuterie board or roasted vegetables, using it as a base for homemade salad dressings or as a dip for crusty bread. “Look for ‘extra virgin’ on the label because this designation indicates a higher polyphenol content,” she says.

3. Oats

Oats are one of the richest dietary sources of a specific type of soluble fiber called beta-glucan. In the digestive tract, beta-glucan forms a thick, gel-like substance that helps remove excess cholesterol from the body. Research suggests that minimally processed oat foods, like steel-cut oats and oat bran, may be especially effective because they retain more of the oat’s natural fiber structure.

Interestingly, liquid oat products sometimes produce more consistent cholesterol-lowering effects than solid oat foods. Researchers think this may be because the beta-glucan in liquids can become more viscous (gel-like) in the digestive tract, which helps trap cholesterol more effectively.

There are so many delicious ways to enjoy oats. Gila Singer, M.S., RD, CDN, FAND, recommends making oatmeal topped with fruit or cinnamon, adding oats or oat milk to smoothies and baked goods like muffins and energy bites, and using oat milk as the base for overnight oats topped with berries and seeds.

4. Nuts

Nuts may help support healthy cholesterol levels thanks to their combination of unsaturated fats, fiber, plant sterols and antioxidant compounds. “Nuts are rich in healthy monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fats,” says Jackie Bridson, M.A., RDN. Replacing foods that are high in saturated fat with nuts like almonds, walnuts and peanuts may help lower LDL cholesterol while supporting overall heart health. The healthy fats found in nuts can help improve blood lipid profiles, while fiber and plant sterols may help reduce cholesterol absorption in the digestive tract. Some nuts, like walnuts, also provide omega-3 fatty acids and polyphenols that may help reduce inflammation and protect blood vessels from oxidative stress associated with cardiovascular disease.

Almonds contain plant sterols, which work by competing with cholesterol in the gut and reducing how much gets absorbed into the bloodstream. “They provide a whopping 6 grams of protein per serving and they can fit into any meal or snack,” says Bonnie Taub-Dix, RDN. “Toss them in oatmeal, sprinkle them over a salad, and keep a small bag in your bag for when hunger hits between meetings.”

Nut butters are another delicious way to enjoy the heart-healthy benefits of nuts. Caroline Young, M.S., RD, RYT, says, “My favorite ways to eat peanut butter are stirred into oatmeal or yogurt bowls or drizzled on top of pancakes with slices of banana.”

5. Lentils

“Lentils are high in soluble fiber and plant protein, both of which contribute to lower total and LDL cholesterol,” notes Singer. She recommends adding them to soups or stews, using them as a base for salads with olive oil and vegetables, or substituting them for meat in dishes like tacos or pasta sauce.

Christina Badaracco, M.P.H., RDN, LDN, notes, “A half-cup of green lentils offers 9 grams of fiber, which helps to remove extra cholesterol from the blood. They also offer protein, manganese, iron and folate, supporting various areas of health and promoting satiety.”

6. Ground Flaxseed

Flaxseeds may be small, but they pack several heart-healthy compounds into a small serving. They’re rich in plant-based omega-3 fats, soluble fiber and antioxidant-rich plant compounds called lignans, all of which may help support healthy cholesterol levels.

“Stir a tablespoon into oatmeal or yogurt, fold it into muffin batter or homemade protein balls, or mix it into a morning smoothie,” says Ormsby. Alyssa Smolen, M.S., RDN, CDN, adds, “One tablespoon of flaxseeds provides almost 3 grams of fiber. Since whole flaxseeds can pass through the digestive tract intact, ground flaxseeds are generally better absorbed.” Because of this, she recommends buying ground flaxseed or grinding whole flaxseeds at home.

Other Tips for Healthy Cholesterol Levels

In addition to incorporating more cholesterol-lowering foods into your pantry, everyday lifestyle habits can also play an important role in heart health.

  • Regular exercise: Adding in movement can help lower LDL cholesterol and raise HDL cholesterol. “Aim for at least 150 minutes of moderate activity per week (like brisk walking),” says Singer.
  • Adequate sleep: “It’s often forgotten that getting adequate sleep, managing stress and moving your body also go a long way when it comes to supporting healthy cholesterol levels,” says Young. A growing body of research suggests that getting adequate sleep may play an important role in maintaining healthy cholesterol levels and supporting overall cardiovascular health, with studies finding that sleep duration and sleep quality may influence lipid metabolism.
  • Meet the recommended daily fiber intake: Focusing on consistent fiber intake each day helps reduce LDL by binding bile acids and increasing their excretion. “Overall, the biggest impact tends to come from dietary patterns, not isolated foods. I always emphasize eating a balanced plate with high fiber intake (aiming for around 25 grams per day for women and around 30 grams per day for men),” says Katz.
  • Swap out saturated fats: Singer recommends limiting saturated fats (found in foods like butter and fatty meats) and opting for unsaturated fats (found in olive oil, nuts, seeds and avocados) when you can. Davis suggests, “If your typical breakfast is eggs on buttered toast, try avocado on your toast instead of butter. If you usually use butter to cook foods like eggs or sautéed veggies, try olive oil or canola oil instead. If you have beef multiple nights per week for dinner, try finding a new salmon recipe and swapping that for beef one night per week.”

Our Expert Take

Supporting healthy cholesterol levels doesn’t have to mean completely overhauling your diet. Pantry staples like beans, extra-virgin olive oil, oats, nuts, lentils and ground flaxseed can provide nutrients that help support heart health in practical and approachable ways. Combined with other healthy lifestyle habits and regular checkups, making these small tweaks and being consistent over time can have a meaningful impact on long-term cardiovascular health.
item.author: EatingWell

item.title: 42岁的女性，在开车时患有脑动脉瘤和中风。最初有2种突发症状- TODAY.com
item.description:
Nichole Vallette woke up on Tuesday, April 21, feeling like her usual self. The 42-year-old mom got ready for work, said goodbye to her husband and started driving to her office in a suburb of Detroit, Michigan.

A few hours later, she would be in brain surgery fighting for her life due to an aneurysm that had no warning signs before it burst.

“It was just a regular morning. I work late afternoon shifts, so she woke me up before she left and said have a good day and I love you,” her husband, Jacob Vallette, tells TODAY.com.

Up until that day, Nichole Vallette had been healthy. The couple, who have three children, live an active life and are eagerly awaiting the birth of their first grandson in July.

Jacob Vallette, 52, always makes sure his wife has made it to work safely before he falls back asleep. On the morning of April 21, she was taking longer than usual to text him back.

“Suddenly, she calls me and tells me she had pulled off the freeway and had thrown up all over,” he says.

Severe Headache and Nausea

While driving, Nichole Vallette suddenly started feeling nauseous and developed an excruciating headache. Panicked, she pulled off the highway and made it to a gas station before vomiting in the car.

After calling out sick to work, Nichole Vallette frantically dialed her husband. The pain in her head was unbearable, she told him.

“I asked her if she thought she should call 911, and she said she didn’t know, but she was just yelling, ‘Ow, my head hurts so bad, it hurts so bad’,” Jacob Vallette recalls.

While pleading with his wife to call 911, she suddenly hung up. When he called back, she didn’t answer. Knowing his wife’s route to work, he decided to get in the car to try to find her.

Finally, his phone rang. “It was the Detroit Fire Department asking who she was because they found her unresponsive.”

Nichole Vallette was transported to a hospital in Detroit, where doctors discovered she had a ruptured aneurysm in her brain.

An aneurysm occurs when an artery, a type of blood vessel carrying oxygen-rich blood to the brain, balloons or bulges due to a weak spot in the arterial wall, per the Cleveland Clinic.

Most aneurysms are small and won’t cause issues. However, when an aneurysm tears or ruptures, this can cause bleeding in the brain, which can lead to a life-threatening hemorrhagic stroke.

Signs of a ruptured aneurysm include an agonizing “thunderclap” headache, nausea, vomiting, blurry vision and sensitivity to light, according to the Brain Aneurysm Foundation.

Brain Surgery

When Nichole Vallette arrived at the hospital, she was unresponsive and seizing, her husband says. Shortly after, she was transferred to another hospital to get brain surgery to relieve the swelling in her brain and treat the aneurysm.

“I didn’t actually set eyes on her until she came out of surgery that night, probably at 11 p.m.,” says Jacob Vallette.

When he finally saw his wife in the ICU, her head was shaved and she was missing part of her skull. “They had to remove a pretty big portion of her skull to allow her brain to swell,” he says.

Nichole Vallette woke up on a ventilator and feeding tube, with three drains in her head. “The first night was pretty rough after surgery, but the next day she was aware of me and everybody in the room,” her husband says.

“We tried to tell her what happened, and she couldn’t talk, but she could squeeze my hand.”

After a few days, Nichole Vallette had the ventilator and feeding tube removed. She could finally speak, but slowly. The stroke left her unable to move her left side.

“After a few days, she started doing real good. Every time we saw a doctor, they were like, she’s doing much better than expected,” her husband says.

Doctors explained that the aneurysm likely happened because of a congenital condition affecting the blood vessels in her brain, which went unnoticed for decades.

Arteriovenous Malformation

Millions of Americans live with unruptured brain aneurysms, often without knowing it. That’s because most brain aneurysms don’t have warning signs, so they’re hard to detect early.

In Nichole Vallette’s case, the aneurysm was caused by an arteriovenous malformation (AVM) in her brain that she’s likely had since birth, her husband says.

An AVM is a abnormal tangle of blood vessels, which creates irregular connections between the arteries and veins in the brain, per the Mayo Clinic. Over time, this can weaken the blood vessels, increasing the risk of an aneurysm.

“They said it’s something people are born with, and sometimes it never affects them. They can live their entire life don’t even know it, and other times we get what happened with Nichole,” Jacob Vallette says.

The symptoms of an AVM can range from headaches to seizures, numbness and confusion, according to the Cleveland Clinic. Some people, like Nichole Vallette, have no signs. Many AVMs are discovered accidentally during a brain scan for another issue.

“They never really had any reason to give her (a scan) before, and they don’t just give head scans to everyone all the time,” Jacob Vallette says.

Aside from the aneurysm, Nichole Vallette had no major health issues, her husband says. “It’s the craziest thing we’ve ever been through.”

“Clawing Her Way Out of This”

One week after the aneurysm, Nichole Vallette was able to stand on her feet with the help of a nurse. She started talking more and even laughing with her sister.

After recovering in the ICU, she was transferred to a rehabilitation hospital in Detroit, where she’s been since early May. The portion of her skull doctors removed is still out and won’t be put back in for a few months, until her brain has fully healed.

In the meantime, Nichole Vallette has been working hard with her care team to regain movement and strength. “She does physical therapy six days a week for three to five hours,” her husband says.

She’s gone from not being able to move one side of her body to walking with a cane and reaching for things with her left hand. Although she’s lost some physical capabilities, she has fully retained her sense of humor and sarcasm, her husband says.

“This girl is clawing her way out of this,” Jacob Vallette wrote in a Facebook post documenting her recovery. “Every day is better, but there are some good days and some bad days.”

Although her long-term memory is intact, she still struggles with feeling cloudy and forgets things in the short term. It’ll take a while for her to regain certain functions. “She wants to come home so bad and misses her cats,” he says.

In a few weeks, she’ll either transfer to another facility or receive in-home rehabilitation care because her husband isn’t able to provide 24-hour care while working full-time.

Among those who survive ruptured brain aneurysms, about 66% suffer lasting neurological deficits, per the Brain Aneurysm Foundation.

“The prognosis is different for everybody. They just said it takes time. It could be six months to a year, maybe 18 months before she reaches her full potential,” says Jacob Vallette, adding that their family is remaining very positive.

“I know that I’m going to have Nichole back, 100%.”

The couple is looking forward to meeting (and spoiling) their first grandson soon. Before the aneurysm, they had bought tickets to a punk rock concert in September, which they still hope to attend — minus any moshing.
item.author: TODAY.com

item.title: ALS正在偷窃他们的尸体。这些父母正在为保持父母身份而斗争- TODAY.com
item.description:
In the summer of 2014, the Ice Bucket Challenge swept across social media with the force of a tidal wave. Celebrities shrieked beneath buckets of freezing water. Teenagers nominated classmates. Grandparents challenged grandchildren. More than 17 million people participated worldwide, raising $220 million for ALS research and briefly turning a little-known neurological disease into one of the most recognizable acronyms in America.

And yet, more than a decade later, many people who dumped ice water over their heads still struggle to explain what ALS actually is.

Several people living with ALS, all interviewed for this story, admitted they had participated in the challenge themselves years earlier, long before their own diagnoses. At the time, they said, they barely understood the disease beyond the viral videos flooding their feeds. They certainly never imagined it could happen to them.

For decades, ALS — amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, also known as Lou Gehrig’s disease — carried the reputation of being an “old person’s disease,” something associated with elderly men.

Although the average age of diagnosis falls between 50 and 65, ALS can strike people in their 20s, 30s and 40s. According to two research studies, Military veterans face nearly double the risk of developing it later in life. And while roughly 90 percent of cases appear sporadically, with no known family history, the disease does not discriminate by race, gender or geography.

Although ALS is considered a rare disease, more than 5,000 Americans are diagnosed with it each year, according to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, and an estimated 30,000 to 35,000 people in the United States are currently living with the illness.

“It affects white people, brown people, young people, old people,” said Dr. Jinsy Andrews, a neurologist and ALS specialist at NYU Langone Health, in an interview with TODAY.com.

A 2025 projection study estimated that the number of Americans living with ALS could rise by more than 10 percent by 2030, an increase that some researchers believe cannot be explained by aging populations alone. Andrews said scientists are increasingly investigating whether environmental exposures, including pollutants, pesticides and other toxins, may interact with genetics in ways that help trigger the disease later in life.

ALS has clarified my priorities and helped me let go of the things that used to feel urgent but weren’t truly important. As a husband and father, it’s reshaped my focus toward connection, honesty, and making the most of the time we as a family have together.

Brian Wallach, a Father of 2 Who was Diagnosed with ALs At age 37

ALS is a progressive neurodegenerative disease that attacks the motor neurons controlling voluntary movement. Over time, patients lose the ability to walk, lift their arms, swallow, speak and eventually breathe on their own, all while the mind remains fully intact, as TODAY previously reported.

There is still no cure, and existing treatments can only modestly slow progression for some patients. Most people die within two to five years of symptom onset, often after the muscles responsible for breathing begin to fail.

A small percentage of patients, however, live significantly longer. About 10% survive more than a decade after diagnosis, and roughly 5% live 20 years or more, according to data from Saint Louis University’s ALS Research Center.

For the first time in decades, researchers say there are genuine reasons for hope. New gene-targeted therapies have shown promise and scientists may finally be learning how to alter the course of ALS, at least for certain forms of the disease, Andrews says.

“It’s the first therapy that raised hopes that we can make ALS a livable or chronic condition,” Andrews says. “I feel like it’s right at our fingertips.”

TODAY.com is spotlighting eight parents navigating the impossible reality of raising children while battling a relentless neurodegenerative disease.

By telling their stories, these moms and dads hope to do more than raise awareness. They are pushing for more research, more funding, better treatments and more time with their families.

Because ALS is still always fatal.

And they are fighting to change that.

Katie Silvestri, 37

Katie Silvestri was the kind of nurse who shaved a homeless man from head to toe because a body lice infestation had become so severe that scratching had opened wounds across his skin.

She showered him herself, covered him in lotion, helped him through quarantine at the height of the COVID pandemic in New York City and, by the end of his isolation period, helped him secure housing.

“She probably quite literally saved his life,” her husband, Dr. David Silvestri, tells TODAY.com.

During the pandemic, Katie, then an intensive care nurse practitioner who previously worked for years as an emergency room nurse, helped oversee the clinical operations of several hotels that temporarily housed medically fragile homeless New Yorkers during quarantine.

Diagnosed with ALS at 34, just three months after giving birth to the couple’s daughter, Sophia, Katie can no longer practice nursing. The family now lives in Falmouth, Massachusetts, where Dave — once on a more traditional physician track — shifted into a remote healthcare executive role in order to help manage the around-the-clock realities of ALS care while raising their now-2-year-old daughter.

Katie communicates using an eye-gaze speech device mounted to her wheelchair; her daughter rides on her lap. At night, they curl up together to watch movies in bed. During living-room dance parties — always to Taylor Swift — Sophia spins around her mother’s chair.

“Everything is so much more special post diagnosis,” Katie tells TODAY.com in responses typed through her device. “Milestones, special moments in the yard, going to the beach, even small phrases from her that make us laugh.”

Sophia has adapted to her mother’s illness with startling tenderness. She helps stretch her mother’s wrists and legs. She mimics caregivers preparing medications. Sometimes she tries pulling on adult-sized medical gloves to help with suctioning equipment.

For Katie, the hardest part of parenting with ALS has not been the physical decline itself, but the moments she misses when Sophia goes off on adventures with friends or family.

“It’s harder for me to get out of the house,” she says, “but it’s important for her to explore and socialize.”

Yet she remains deeply embedded in the invisible labor of motherhood, tracking groceries, allergies, pajamas, room temperatures and favorite snacks through texts, Amazon orders and careful observations.

“She’s still carrying that,” Dave says. “What shoes fit. What clothes she has upstairs. Does she have enough yogurt?””

Recently, a package arrived at the house: a toddler-sized Dyson vacuum, functional and perfectly scaled for a toddler.

“Sophia would not be getting that if it was just me,” Dave says. “Katie knows exactly what she’ll love.”

Melissa Korn, 37

People often direct their questions to Melissa Korn’s husband, Garrett.

Strangers see the healthy spouse standing beside a wheelchair and assume, almost unconsciously, that Garrett has become the center of the family.

Melissa notices it every time.

What she wishes people understood is that while ALS has transformed her body, it has not displaced her from the role she still occupies most fully: being a mother.

“I’m still the one keeping track of everything,” Korn, 37, tells TODAY.com from her home outside Atlanta where she and Garrett are raising their two sons, Theo, 5, and Weston, 3. “I still know where everybody needs to be. I still talk to the teachers. That hasn’t changed.”

Nearly three years ago, Korn was healthy and active when she began noticing weakness that felt impossible to explain. Weston was just seven months old. At first, she assumed it was postpartum exhaustion. But she struggled getting up from the floor after playing with the boys. At the gym, weights suddenly felt heavier. Then she started falling.

The moment that frightened her most came near a swimming pool, when she stumbled backward while carrying one of her sons up the stairs.

“Garrett saw it and said, ‘OK. We need to look into this,’” she recalls.

The diagnosis — ALS — rearranged nearly every practical aspect of family life. Korn gradually lost the ability to walk and much of the use of her arms. Garrett became her primary caregiver while also parenting two young children.

He cooks now. Cleans. Folds laundry.

But Melissa still carries the mental load of motherhood.

She is still tracking school schedules, doctors’ appointments, carpools, and monitoring what snacks need to be replenished in the cupboard. She’s still the one reminding Garrett where the boys need to be and when.

Before ALS, Korn understood motherhood through action: packing lunches, carrying children, and driving them to their grandparents. Losing those abilities forced her to redefine what being a good parent means.

Now, she says, motherhood feels less physical and more emotional.

“It’s making them feel safe and loved,” she says.

Friday movie nights continue with pizza and all four family members piled into bed together. Theo and Weston still reach for their mother when they are scared. Korn tries carefully not to let grief become the emotional center of the family.

Recently, Theo, old enough to remember life before the disease, said something to her that she still struggles to talk about.

“He told me, ‘I miss you walking,’” Korn says quietly.

She told him the truth.

“I said, ‘I miss it too.’”

Brian Wallach, 45

Brian Wallach had spent much of his life building momentum. After graduating from Yale and Georgetown Law, he worked on Barack Obama’s presidential campaign and later served in the Obama White House before building a successful legal career and starting a family with his wife, Sandra Abrevaya.

Then, at 37, he was diagnosed with ALS, a disease that changed nearly every part of his life, but not his determination to keep moving forward.

He received the diagnosis in 2017, the same day he and Abrevaya brought home their second daughter from the hospital. Doctors told him he might have six months to live.

Eight years later, he is still here.

Wallach, who lives in Illinois, communicates through eye gaze technology, a system that allows people with paralysis to use a computer using only their eyes. Despite the painstaking process, eye gaze allows him to continue parenting his two daughters, ages 8 and 10.

“We watch movies together and play games,” he tells TODAY.com. “Some things may look different than I once imagined, but I am still present for their sporting events, plays, and school activities.”

This past March, the family went skiing together using adaptive equipment.

“It’s a favorite shared activity we have,” he says.

The disease has sharpened his understanding of what matters.

“In a surprising way, ALS has given me freedom,” Wallach says. “ALS has clarified my priorities and helped me let go of the things that used to feel urgent but weren’t truly important. As a husband and father, it’s reshaped my focus toward connection, honesty, and making the most of the time we as a family have together.”

That clarity transformed his public life, too. Together, Wallach and Abrevaya co-founded I AM ALS, a patient-led advocacy organization that helped push Congress to expand ALS research funding and improve access to experimental treatments.

Wallach says his daughters have become his greatest example of resilience.

“My daughters have taught me everything about resilience,” he says. “Their childhood looks very different from their peers, but they never let that define them. They are kind, loving young women, and I could not be more proud to be their dad.”

Abrevaya sees those moments of parenting as the true measure of what her husband has accomplished while living with ALS.

“Our 10-year-old had a setback recently, and Brian ‘spoke’ with her by typing with his eyes on his eye-gaze machine that he was so proud of how she handled the disappointment and kept pushing forward,” she says. “She ran over to tell me what ‘Dad said,’ and was beaming. That’s everything you need to know about how special it is that Brian is not only still alive, but still able to parent as our girls grow up.”

“I’ve been able to see my daughters through so many milestones,” he says. “It brings me immense joy to know I got to be there.”

Salym Liufau, 34

Salym Liufau had come to her daughter Clover’s elementary school for lunch in Houston, Texas, carrying Chick-fil-A. Three years into an ALS diagnosis determined to take away her independence, she is equally determined not to miss the ordinary moments of motherhood she can still hold onto.

Across from her, Clover, 8, chatted with classmates in the noisy cafeteria. Salym had brought food for herself too, though she had not really planned on eating. Since ALS had weakened her arms, even lifting food to her mouth had become difficult.

Clover, a third grader, looked at her mother and gently asked: “Mom, do you want me to help you?”

“Most kids would be embarrassed,” Salym tells TODAY.com. “But she said, ‘I don’t care what anyone thinks.’”

For Salym, who was diagnosed with ALS in 2023, the moment felt enormous.

Three years ago, she never sat still. She spent her days shuttling her children, Lila, 11, Clover, and 5-year-old twins Meadow and Jai, to soccer games, gymnastics classes and playgrounds while her husband, Dan, 36, worked long shifts as a firefighter.

Now, her legs no longer work. She cannot raise her arms high enough to reach her glasses. ALS has taken away nearly every physical task of motherhood: making lunches, tying shoes, bath time. When one of her children falls down, she still instinctively tries to move toward them before remembering she cannot.

“You’re stuck,” she says quietly.

When her children get sick, someone else has to wipe their mouths or hold their hair back. Someone else puts on the Band-Aid. But afterward, they still come looking for her. They climb into her wheelchair for hugs. They curl into her lap for comfort.

“I’m still their mom,” Salym says.

Asked what she would tell herself before her diagnosis, she immediately began to cry.

Before ALS, she says, she always felt the house had to be perfect, the counter clean, every toy picked up before she allowed herself to slow down and sit down on the floor with her children.

Now, that urgency feels painfully misplaced.

“Play the Play-Doh,” she says. “The dishes can wait.”

A destroyed living room, Salym has realized, is not failure. It is evidence of childhood, of time together.

“That means life,” she says. “That’s living.”

Steve Gleason, 49

On the night the Superdome reopened after Hurricane Katrina in 2006, New Orleans was still carrying the devastation of the storm. Entire neighborhoods sat gutted and families were still scattered across the country. And yet, inside the stadium where the New Orleans Saints faced the Atlanta Falcons, more than 70,000 people arrived desperate to feel something other than grief.

Then came the punt.

Steve Gleason, a special teams player for the Saints, exploded through the line and blocked a Falcons punt, which resulted in a touchdown for the Saints. The stadium detonated. Strangers hugged each other and sobbed openly.

The play became a symbol of hope and survival, proof that even after enormous loss, New Orleans could still find its way back to joy.

Years later, Gleason would confront another life-changing moment when he was diagnosed with ALS at 34. But much like the city that once found hope in his blocked punt, Gleason rebuilt his life around a new sense of purpose, becoming one of the country’s most visible ALS advocates.

Since founding Team Gleason in 2011, the organization has provided more than $65 million in technology, equipment, care services and support for people living with ALS.

The diagnosis came in January 2011, shortly after Gleason and his wife, Michel Varisco-Gleason, learned they were expecting their first child, Rivers. Rivers is now 14, and the couple also have a 7-year-old daughter, Gray.

Over time, the disease forced Gleason to rethink nearly everything he once associated with fatherhood.

“At first I thought ‘showing up’ meant being the active dad doing all the physical stuff,” he tells TODAY.com. “As the disease took more, I realized I couldn’t be that guy the way I wanted.”

“Your presence is enough,” he says. “That’s my mantra now.”

Over the years, Gleason says, his children have also reshaped how he sees the world.

One lesson was illustrated by Gray. After a soccer game last fall, Gleason, who still lives in New Orleans, watched as players lined up to shake hands and parents folded up chairs along the sidelines. Gray, meanwhile, noticed dragonflies hovering above the field. She kicked off her cleats and began chasing them barefoot through the grass, unconcerned with the final score or whether anyone was watching.

“Gray reminds me constantly that life is not something to win,” Gleason says. “It’s something to inhabit fully.”

Gleason isn’t coaching teams or throwing spirals with his kids in the backyard. But in place of the version of fatherhood he once pictured came a clearer understanding of what matters most.

“For any parent, I think the lesson is the same,” Gleason says. “Put the phone down, drop the agenda, and just be with your kids. Pay attention. Tell them they’re enough exactly as they are. Aim high with them, but make sure they know your love isn’t conditional on performance.”

Then he distills it even further.

“Presence,” he says. “That’s equal to love.”

Evan Campa, 44

What does it mean to keep mothering when your body is disappearing?

That’s the question Evan Campa has been returning to on her blog since she was diagnosed with ALS in 2018 at 37.

At the time, Evan and her husband, Eben, were living in India as a Foreign Service family with two small children: Mae, then 3, and Reuben, 1. Before the diagnosis, Evan worked in the autism field, directing an autism center while also throwing herself fully into parenting, travel and community-building.

Now 44 and living in Nashville, Evan can’t speak and communicates through a Tobii eye-gaze device. She still parents with the same instinct to give her kids a big, full life, but the way she does it has changed completely.

She answered these interview questions slowly and carefully using the device that has become her voice.

“Before ALS I was very hands-on and involved and eager to take the kids everywhere and introduce them to new experiences,” tells TODAY.com. “Now ALS has forced me to often watch them have experiences from the sidelines. I still take them places and give them new experiences but I’m not the active participant I once was.”

“The hardest part,” she says, “is not being able to have an effortless conversation with them.”

Mae is now 11. Reuben is 9. In the years since her diagnosis, family life has gradually reorganized itself around the disease. The children often turn to Eben, 43, who works in government relations, for the more physical parts of parenting. Which is why the moments that mean the most to Evan now can feel surprisingly small.

“It’s especially meaningful when they come to me for help — it makes me feel worthwhile and like a mom,” she wrote.

Evan says her children have adapted to their reality with a resilience that continues to surprise her.

“They definitely wish I could do more with them and for me to be healed, but they mostly seem to accept our reality and love me as I am and that’s a really special feeling.”

Still, Evan resists flattening her experience into either tragedy or inspiration. Her blog often moves between humor, grief, anger and gratitude.

In one post, she reflected on the quiet grief of losing the physical rituals of motherhood that once came naturally. It had been years, she wrote, since she’d carried her children, fixed a stubborn zipper or read bedtime stories aloud. Watching others step into those caregiving roles brought “a weighty sorrow.”

But over time, she says ALS also reshaped her understanding of what makes a mom “enough.”

Evan began to see meaning in smaller moments: lying beside her daughter listening to audiobooks, her hand resting on her chest; racing her son on her mobility scooter while he sped beside her on his own.

“Countless other moments of connection,” Evan wrote, reminded her that love and attention can matter just as much as doing.

A’Tileana Gipson, 30

Five days before she gave birth to her daughter, at 27 years old, A’Tileana “TT” Gipson learned she was dying.

Doctors had finally found an explanation for the unexplained falls and weakness spreading through her left leg. Then, almost immediately, she had to prepare for labor.

“I received a death sentence,” A’Tileana, now 30, tells TODAY.com, “and I didn’t have any time to process before bringing new life into the world.”

Before her diagnosis, A’Tileana worked in early childhood education and spent weekends chasing her son, Ayden, through playgrounds and school events. She described the two of them as “each other’s entertainment.”

Friends and family saw her as fiercely independent, sharing how she once picked up and moved to a new state by herself without thinking twice.

“She’s fearless,” her mother, Tianna McBroom says.

But A’Tileana is also deeply introverted, something that made what came next especially unlikely.

As her disease progressed, she began posting videos online about life with ALS — something she says took three years to feel brave enough to do.

Eventually, she decided silence felt worse.

Now A’Tileana has become an outspoken advocate for ALS awareness and founded Kiss My ALS, a nonprofit-inspired apparel brand that raises money for ALS research and awareness. The organization describes itself as “a bold voice for the ALS community, refusing to stay quiet.”

At home in Minnesota, Gipson parents her 3-year-old daughter, Amiri, from a power wheelchair. Amiri, she says laughing, has already learned how to use it against her.

“She knows if she goes in certain areas I can’t get her,” Gipson says, laughing. “Her new thing is coloring on the walls. She gets into the corners, and I’ll say, ‘Please hand me the marker,’ and she’ll just stand there and keep coloring.”

Even as the disease progresses, McBroom says her daughter continues to push herself for her children.

“She doesn’t parent from her bed,” McBroom says.

On the days she feels strong enough, Gipson still takes her children to museums, parks and the Mall of America, determined to preserve as much normalcy as she can.

She laughs often, even while describing the parts of motherhood ALS has taken from her. Her 10-year-old son now lives primarily with his father, though Gipson still sees him every weekend and during school breaks.

What she hopes her children carry from all of this, she says, is empathy. “I just hope it creates kind kids.”

Angie Quandt, 56

Before Angie Quandt was diagnosed with ALS at age 52, her social media pages looked like what you might expect from a mother of six.

There are family vacation photos from Disneyland and Hawaii, birthday dinners, graduations and group selfies. In almost every picture, Angie, who lives in Oregon, is surrounded by her husband, Martin, and their children: Chris, 35, Adam, 33, Nick, 31, Megan, 29, Emily, 24, and Ella Grace, 19.

Her kids are almost always hugging her.

Even after her ALS diagnosis, that part has not changed.

“The hardest part of parenting with ALS is reassuring my children that everything will be alright even when I’m not physically here.”

Angie Quandt, a Mother of six Who Was Diagnosed with AlS at 52.

The disease has gradually affected Angie’s physical abilities, but her family remains tightly connected. Her children visit nearly every day and they continue traveling and making memories together.

“We all had to realize how precious time is,” Angie tells TODAY.com.

The family said one of the hardest moments came when Angie and Martin sat their children down to tell them about the ALS diagnosis.

“It was the most difficult thing we’ve had to do as parents,” she says.

Over time, those conversations have become more emotional as the disease has progressed.

“The hardest part of parenting with ALS,” Angie says, “is reassuring my children that everything will be alright even when I’m not physically here.”

“I want to ensure that my kids feel loved and cherished by me, and that they remember all the positive advice and life guidance I’ve told them over the years,” she says.

Since her diagnosis, four grandchildren have been born, with another due in August.

Her daughter Emily says watching her mother become a grandparent has become one of the family’s greatest joys.

Though Angie is unable to safely hold and rock her grandkids to sleep — something Emily says her mother deeply mourned — she has found other ways to shower them with affection, spoiling them with carefully chosen clothes, stuffed animals, blankets and toys.

In recent months, Emily has put her post-graduation plans on pause and taken three months away from work to help care for her mother, a decision she says she will never regret.

The time together, she says, has only strengthened their relationship.

“We have become closer than ever and have so many memories to look back on,” Emily says. “She has taught me the importance of living in the moment and not worrying about the little things.”
